item.title: I tested Gemini in Android Auto for a week, and it changed how I use my car
item.itunes:title: I tested Gemini in Android Auto for a week, and it changed how I use my car
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
When Google first announced Gemini’s integration in Android Auto, it was easy to dismiss it as just another coat of paint on a familiar voice assistant. But after putting it through its paces during my daily commutes, it became clear that I had underestimated the upgrade.

Gemini behind the wheel isn’t just slightly faster or mildly better at understanding accents; it changes the on-the-go computing while you drive.

4 ways I stopped fighting Android Auto and started customizing it instead

Small changes that add up to make a noticeable impact

4
By  Chandraveer Mathur

Gemini in Android Auto

The transition from Google Assistant to Gemini

Initially, I was cynical about the ‘AI everywhere’ push and expected it to be a distraction while driving. However, Gemini proved me wrong.

If your dashboard is still defaulting to the classic Google Assistant, you have to trigger the handoff directly from your phone first. The car’s infotainment system is simply mirroring your phone’s primary assistant ecosystem.

You can also try installing Android 17 developer beta to unlock Gemini on your Android Auto system. Now, Gemini is only as smart as the data it can access. To unlock its true power, you need to head into your phone’s Gemini Settings and enable the Google Workspace and YouTube Music toggles under the Personal Intelligence menu.

Once those settings are dialed in, and you connect to Android Auto, the difference between the old Assistant and Gemini becomes obvious. Let’s check it in action.

Gemini understands complex requests

It works effectively too

To see why Gemini feels like a generational leap over Google Assistant, you have to throw a messy, multi-intent prompt at it while moving down the road.

Last week, I fired up Gemini and asked for something like "Take me to Shivani Clean Care and see if there are any Jio petrol pumps on the route," and it handled the query without breaking a sweat. Instead of getting confused by two separate requests crammed into one sentence, Gemini’s underlying architecture maps out a logical execution sequence behind the scenes and mimics how a human co-pilot thinks.

Instead of a frustrating "I don’t understand," Gemini updates the dashboard smoothly. It calculates the route to the destination, highlights a Jio-bp station directly along the path, and responds with natural audio: ‘Navigating to Shivani Clean Care. I found a Jio-bp station right along your route on Varachha road – it adds about two minutes to your drive. Would you like me to add it as a stop?’ You can actually think out loud and be confident that your car is actually listening.

Gemini works with Google Workspace apps

I use it with Docs and Keep Notes

This is where the magic of the Google Workspace extension shines. When you are behind the wheel, your car effectively becomes an extension of your desktop workspace. Just the other day, I was comparing the best FTP clients for a project. I had compiled all my notes, technical specs, and software into a structured Google Doc.

Later, while driving, I realized I needed a clean checklist for those specific tools ready to go the moment I stepped back into my office, so I could start testing them one by one. Instead of waiting until I parked, pulling out my phone, and awkwardly copying and pasting text, I handled the entire cross-app workflow using nothing but my voice through Android Auto. I asked, "Open my recent document about the best FTP clients, extract all the software tools listed in it, and create a checklist in Google Keep." By the time I pulled into my driveway and walked up to my desk, the heavy lifting was already done.

Finding songs with Gemini in Android Auto

YouTube Music integration is spot on

Perhaps the most satisfying element of using Gemini behind the wheel is YouTube Music integration. Just the other day, a specific melody was stuck in my head while I was driving, but I couldn’t remember the name of the track. All I knew was its context. I hit the steering wheel button and said, "Play that intro song from the Dhurandhar movie."

Gemini took that vague descriptor, treated it as a query, and within two seconds, it figured out exactly what my brain was struggling to recall: it identified ‘Ishq Jalakar’ as the opening track, loaded it via the YouTube Music extension, and the track started playing over my car’s speakers. It gets even better. If you don’t even have the context of a movie or an album name, Gemini can parse audio characteristics. If a tune is floating around in your head, you can just invoke the assistant and hum the melody directly to your dashboard.

Don’t underestimate Gemini in your car

Ultimately, Gemini’s integration into Android Auto proves that the most valuable AI isn’t one that writes poetry or codes a website from scratch; it’s the one that handles the logistical chaos of real life. With a switch from Google Assistant to Gemini, Google has quietly fixed the most frustrating aspect of the in-car experience.

Gemini delivers exactly what a driver actually needs: a reliable, frictionless, and intelligent co-pilot that lets you keep your hands on the wheel and eyes firmly on the road. Of course, these are just the early days of Gemini in Android Auto. With the support of more apps coming, I can’t wait to see how Google improves it in the future.

4 Android Auto tweaks that transformed my car's infotainment system

You'll notice these changes if they're reversed after the fact

2
By  Chandraveer Mathur

Gemini took that vague descriptor, treated it as a query, and within two seconds, it figured out exactly what my brain was struggling to recall: it identified ‘Ishq Jalakar’ as the opening track, loaded it via the YouTube Music extension, and the track started playing over my car’s speakers. It gets even better. If you don’t even have the context of a movie or an album name, Gemini can parse audio characteristics. If a tune is floating around in your head, you can just invoke the assistant and hum the melody directly to your dashboard.

Don’t underestimate Gemini in your car

Ultimately, Gemini’s integration into Android Auto proves that the most valuable AI isn’t one that writes poetry or codes a website from scratch; it’s the one that handles the logistical chaos of real life. With a switch from Google Assistant to Gemini, Google has quietly fixed the most frustrating aspect of the in-car experience.

Gemini delivers exactly what a driver actually needs: a reliable, frictionless, and intelligent co-pilot that lets you keep your hands on the wheel and eyes firmly on the road. Of course, these are just the early days of Gemini in Android Auto. With the support of more apps coming, I can’t wait to see how Google improves it in the future.

4 Android Auto tweaks that transformed my car's infotainment system

You'll notice these changes if they're reversed after the fact

2
By  Chandraveer Mathur

item.title: Powerline Ethernet is ugly, imperfect, and sometimes exactly what your house needs
item.itunes:title: Powerline Ethernet is ugly, imperfect, and sometimes exactly what your house needs
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you live in a home with thick plaster walls, concrete floors, or multi-story architectural barriers, you might find that your high-end Wi-Fi keeps dropping the connection, and a wireless mesh node just repeats a degraded signal. You can't drill holes because you rent, or because your landlord would lose their mind.

What is the solution here? The unlikely savior is a bulky, unappealing plastic brick into a wall outlet near your router. Plug another one into your bedroom, and suddenly you have a hardwired internet connection running over your building's copper power grid. Yes, powerline Ethernet is ugly, technically flawed, and completely breaks the rules of elegant network design. Despite its imperfections, it remains the ultimate problem-solver for complex structural layouts, offering a rock-solid alternative when every other networking option fails. It might be a messy compromise, and it will never hit its advertised box speeds, and a vacuum cleaner could cause your ping to spike. When Wi-Fi cannot punch through structural walls and running Ethernet is out of the question, the powerline provides a stable, low-jitter connection exactly where you need it.

Your home server needs Ethernet, but not for the reason you think

Stability over speed, always.

1
By  Samir Makwana

How does powerline communication work?

It's not entirely elegant

The basic concept of powerline communication, otherwise known as PLC, is the piggyback principle. It introduces data packets into your home's existing electrical wiring by modulating a high-frequency digital signal onto the baseline 50 Hz or 60 Hz alternating current (AC) power waveform. Essentially, it can transmit data packets over your home's electrical wiring, allowing you to replicate an internet signal.

While it is a great alternative, there are some blatant marketing claims on the packaging of adapters labeled AV2 2000 that promise 2000Mbps throughput. That is a theoretical aggregate physical layer speed. In a real home, a reader will be lucky to see 200–400 Mbps of usable TCP throughput. Don't trust the specifications on the box, because powerline Ethernet isn't 100% reliable in this regard.

There are some physical bottlenecks that can dictate whether or not powerline will succeed or fail in your home. Modern homes split the electrical supply into two 120V phases within the main breaker panel. If your router is plugged into an outlet on phase A and your PC is on phase B, then the data packet has to travel all the way back to the breaker box and cross over to the other phase panel, which can severely degrade speeds. It won't make it completely unusable, but it may as well be, thanks to how low the speeds will be.

Another issue you might encounter is the appliance tax, which can cause significant signal noise. Home wiring has no shielding against electromagnetic interference (EMI). This means devices with heavy electric motors or cheap power supplies, such as vacuum cleaners, phone chargers, treadmills, and hair dryers, can dump massive amounts of line noise into the copper. When they turn on, your powerline bandwidth drops, and your latency can spike.

Don't ignore the obvious solution

It might not be perfect, but it might solve your problems

Despite its flaws, a powerline wins in a wide range of scenarios. It can legitimately outperform expensive Wi-Fi in many homes. It can be great for casual and competitive gamers alike. While Wi-Fi 7 can hit crazy peak speeds, airwaves are prone to environmental packet loss, which can cause jitter. This means that, despite having higher overall speeds, your ping can be pretty inconsistent, which is completely essential for gaming, especially competitively. PowerLine might have lower peak speeds overall, but its baseline packet delivery is remarkably consistent, making it superior for a lag-free experience in games where low ping is essential.

Another great example where Powerline wins out is in an isolated home lab or home office. If you host a local server or just need a reliable connection for work video calls in a detached garage or a distant basement bedroom, Powerline establishes a continuous link that completely ignores physical walls, making it perfect for these environments.

It's also great for a dedicated smart home hub backhaul. Placing a local automation controller like Home Assistant in a central room often means moving away from your main router. PowerLine serves as a perfect hardwired backhaul to keep your local hub connected to the primary local area network (LAN).

There are some golden rules for making powerline actually work. If you are planning to set it up, ensure you keep these in mind to avoid the most common user mistakes. First things first, ensure it's directly into the wall. Never, under any circumstances, plug a power adapter into a surge protector or extension cord. The metal oxide varistors inside surge protectors treat high-frequency data packets as an electrical surge and actively filter them out, which instantly kills your connection and will make your powerline adapter useless.

Subscribe for practical powerline Ethernet tips

Sign up for the newsletter to receive clear, practical guidance on powerline Ethernet - setup rules, interference fixes, realistic speed expectations, and when to choose it over Wi-Fi so you can decide confidently.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Also, avoid GFCIs. Outlets protected by ground-fault circuit interrupters or arc-fault circuit interrupters feature internal coils that interpret PLC data signals as dangerous electrical arcs, and they can occasionally trip the breaker entirely or strangle the bandwidth.

You don't need to spend hundreds on a new router

Powerline Ethernet is a great middle ground

Networking isn't always about chasing bleeding-edge enterprise perfection. It's about deploying the right tool to solve an immediate, messy, real-world problem.

Stop looking at powerline as a failed tech product because it doesn't match Cat 6 speeds. If you're trapped behind a literal wall of concrete, put down the expensive mesh router brochures and spend $60 on a dual-pack home plug AV2 kit, and let your home's copper wiring do the heavy lifting. It might not be a fantastic, completely elegant solution, but it's an absolutely perfect temporary one if you're in a situation where drilling into your walls just isn't an option.

Nexuslink G.hn Powerline Ethernet Kit

If you're looking for a way to access ethernet without drilling into your walls or drapping cables across your entire home, then powerline ethernet is a great option.

$60 at Amazon
